---
title: "Chapter 27 — 👁️ Computer Vision"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 26 · ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory](ch26.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [28 · 💬 Natural Language Processing →](ch28.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Computer vision is the subfield of AI that teaches machines to extract meaning from images and video — to turn a grid of pixel intensities into answers like "that's a pedestrian," "the tumor is here," or "this is the same face." It sits at the intersection of signal processing, machine learning, and deep learning, and it was the first domain where deep neural networks decisively beat hand-engineered methods, kicking off the modern AI era. This chapter walks the field from its classical pipeline through the core tasks practitioners actually ship.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Part 7 (Language, Vision & Modalities) · turning pixels into structured predictions — labels, boxes, masks, keypoints · the key idea: learn features from data instead of hand-crafting them.
:::

## 27.1 — The classical pipeline and the deep-learning shift

For decades, vision systems followed a fixed assembly line: **acquire** an image, **preprocess** it (resize, denoise, normalize lighting), extract **features** (compact numeric descriptors of texture, edges, corners), then feed those features to a **model** (an SVM, a random forest) that makes the decision. The intelligence lived almost entirely in the feature step, and that step was designed by hand.

The two most famous hand-crafted descriptors are **SIFT** (Scale-Invariant Feature Transform) and **HOG** (Histogram of Oriented Gradients). The shared intuition is that raw pixel values are fragile — shift the image one pixel, brighten it, rotate it slightly, and every number changes — but *gradients* (local changes in intensity, i.e. edges) are far more stable. HOG, for example, slices the image into small cells, computes the dominant edge direction in each, and builds a histogram of those directions. A histogram doesn't care exactly where inside the cell the edge sits, which buys robustness to small shifts.

The trouble is that a human had to guess which patterns mattered, and those guesses didn't transfer: a descriptor tuned for pedestrians was useless for diagnosing X-rays. The shift came in 2012 when a deep **convolutional neural network** (AlexNet) won the ImageNet classification contest by a wide margin. The lesson was that the feature step should be *learned*, not designed — the network discovers its own edge detectors in early layers and abstract object parts in deeper layers, all optimized end-to-end for the actual task.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Acquire] --> B[Preprocess]
  B --> C{Features}
  C -->|classical| D[SIFT / HOG hand-crafted]
  C -->|deep| E[Learned conv filters]
  D --> F[SVM / RF]
  E --> G[Neural net head]
  F --> H[Prediction]
  G --> H
```

::: {.callout-tip}
A useful way to read the whole history: classical CV separates *feature extraction* from *classification*; deep learning fuses them into one differentiable function trained jointly. Everything downstream in this chapter is a variation on "what head do we attach to learned features?"
:::

The mechanics of convolution, pooling, and conv-net architectures are owned by Chapter 16 — here we treat a trained CNN (or Vision Transformer) as a **backbone**: a function that maps an image to a stack of feature maps, which the task-specific *heads* below consume.

## 27.2 — Image classification

Classification is the canonical "hello world" of vision: given a whole image, output a single label from a fixed set — *cat*, *dog*, *fire truck*. It's the simplest task because it asks only *what* is in the image, not *where*.

A modern classifier is a backbone that compresses the image into a feature vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$, followed by a linear layer and a **softmax** that turns raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution over the $K$ classes:

$$
p_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}, \qquad \mathcal{L} = -\log p_{y}
$$

The loss is the negative log-probability of the true class $y$ — **cross-entropy** — which pushes the network to put mass on the correct label. At inference you take the $\arg\max$.

```python
import numpy as np

def softmax(z):
    z = z - z.max()                 # subtract max for numerical stability
    e = np.exp(z)
    return e / e.sum()

def cross_entropy(logits, y):
    p = softmax(logits)
    return -np.log(p[y] + 1e-12)    # loss = -log prob of true class

logits = np.array([2.0, 0.5, -1.0])  # scores for 3 classes
assert np.isclose(softmax(logits).sum(), 1.0)
assert cross_entropy(logits, 0) < cross_entropy(logits, 2)  # right class cheaper
```

Two practical wrinkles matter. First, **top-5 accuracy** (is the true label among the five highest-scoring?) is often reported alongside top-1, because many images genuinely contain ambiguity. Second, classification assumes exactly one dominant subject; for images with several objects you want detection (next section), and for mutually-non-exclusive labels (a photo that is both *outdoor* and *beach*) you swap softmax for independent per-label **sigmoids** — multi-label classification.

## 27.3 — Object detection: boxes, IoU, mAP, and NMS

Detection answers *what* **and** *where*: it outputs a list of **bounding boxes**, each with a class label and confidence. The headache is that the number of objects varies per image, so the network can't just emit a fixed-length vector.

To even define "correct," we need a way to score a predicted box against a ground-truth box. That's **IoU** (Intersection over Union): the area of overlap divided by the area of their union. IoU = 1 is a perfect match; a common threshold is IoU ≥ 0.5 to count a detection as a hit.

<svg viewBox="0 0 200 110" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <rect x="20" y="20" width="80" height="60" fill="none" stroke="#6aa9ff" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="60" y="40" width="80" height="60" fill="none" stroke="#ff8a65" stroke-width="2"/>
  <rect x="60" y="40" width="40" height="40" fill="#ffd54f" fill-opacity="0.5"/>
  <text x="22" y="16" font-size="9" fill="#6aa9ff">ground truth</text>
  <text x="105" y="108" font-size="9" fill="#ff8a65">prediction</text>
  <text x="62" y="34" font-size="8">∩ = yellow</text>
</svg>

```python
def iou(a, b):                       # boxes as (x1, y1, x2, y2)
    ix1, iy1 = max(a[0], b[0]), max(a[1], b[1])
    ix2, iy2 = min(a[2], b[2]), min(a[3], b[3])
    inter = max(0, ix2 - ix1) * max(0, iy2 - iy1)
    area_a = (a[2]-a[0]) * (a[3]-a[1])
    area_b = (b[2]-b[0]) * (b[3]-b[1])
    return inter / (area_a + area_b - inter + 1e-9)

assert iou((0,0,2,2), (0,0,2,2)) == 1.0
assert iou((0,0,2,2), (1,1,3,3)) < 0.2   # 1/7 overlap
```

A detector typically fires *many* overlapping boxes for the same object. **NMS** (Non-Maximum Suppression) cleans this up: keep the highest-confidence box, delete every other box that overlaps it past an IoU threshold, repeat.

```python
def nms(boxes, scores, thr=0.5):
    order = sorted(range(len(boxes)), key=lambda i: scores[i], reverse=True)
    keep = []
    while order:
        i = order.pop(0)             # take current highest score
        keep.append(i)
        order = [j for j in order if iou(boxes[i], boxes[j]) < thr]  # drop overlaps
    return keep
```

To score a whole detector across confidence thresholds, the field uses **mAP** (mean Average Precision): for each class, sweep the confidence threshold, trace out a precision–recall curve, take the area under it (Average Precision), then average over classes. COCO further averages mAP over IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95.

Architecturally, detectors split into two families:

| Family | Examples | How it works | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two-stage | R-CNN, Fast/Faster R-CNN | First propose regions, then classify + refine each | More accurate, slower |
| One-stage | YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet | Predict boxes + classes in a single forward pass over a grid | Faster, real-time, historically slightly less accurate |

The R-CNN lineage tells the optimization story: original **R-CNN** ran a CNN on ~2000 cropped proposals (painfully slow); **Fast R-CNN** ran the CNN once and pooled features per region; **Faster R-CNN** replaced the external proposal generator with a learned **Region Proposal Network**. Most of these methods lean on **anchors** — a fixed grid of reference boxes of assorted sizes and aspect ratios — and the network only learns to *adjust* an anchor and label it, which is far easier than predicting boxes from scratch. Newer detectors like **DETR** drop anchors and NMS entirely, posing detection as a set-prediction problem solved with a Transformer.

::: {.callout-warning}
High overall accuracy can hide a broken small-object detector. Always inspect mAP broken down by object size — small objects (distant pedestrians, tiny lesions) routinely score far worse, and a single aggregate number masks it.
:::

## 27.4 — Segmentation: semantic, instance, and panoptic

Detection draws rectangles; segmentation classifies *every pixel*. There are three flavors, and confusing them is a classic interview trap.

- **Semantic segmentation** labels each pixel with a class but doesn't separate instances — all cars get the "car" label, merged into one blob.
- **Instance segmentation** labels each pixel *and* separates objects — car #1 vs car #2 — but typically only for countable "thing" classes.
- **Panoptic segmentation** unifies both: every pixel gets a class, "stuff" (sky, road) is labeled semantically and "things" (cars, people) are separated by instance.

<svg viewBox="0 0 300 90" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <text x="0" y="12" font-size="9">semantic</text>
  <rect x="0" y="18" width="40" height="40" fill="#6aa9ff"/>
  <rect x="40" y="18" width="40" height="40" fill="#6aa9ff"/>
  <text x="0" y="78" font-size="8">cars = one blob</text>
  <text x="110" y="12" font-size="9">instance</text>
  <rect x="110" y="18" width="40" height="40" fill="#ff8a65"/>
  <rect x="150" y="18" width="40" height="40" fill="#ffd54f"/>
  <text x="110" y="78" font-size="8">car1 / car2 split</text>
  <text x="220" y="12" font-size="9">panoptic</text>
  <rect x="220" y="18" width="80" height="40" fill="#aed581"/>
  <rect x="222" y="20" width="36" height="36" fill="#ff8a65"/>
  <rect x="260" y="20" width="36" height="36" fill="#ffd54f"/>
  <text x="220" y="78" font-size="8">stuff + things</text>
</svg>

The workhorse for semantic segmentation is **U-Net**: an encoder that downsamples the image into compact features, a decoder that upsamples back to full resolution, and **skip connections** that splice high-resolution detail from the encoder directly into the matching decoder stage. The intuition is that downsampling tells you *what* but throws away *where*; the skip connections hand back the precise spatial detail the decoder needs to draw crisp boundaries. Originally built for biomedical images, U-Net remains a default far beyond it.

For instance segmentation, **Mask R-CNN** extends Faster R-CNN with a third head: alongside the box and the class, each detected region gets a small **mask** branch that predicts a per-pixel binary foreground map inside the box. Because the masks are predicted per detected instance, objects stay separated for free.

Segmentation quality is usually scored with **IoU per class** (here computed over pixel sets rather than boxes), averaged into **mean IoU (mIoU)**. A recent shift worth knowing: **promptable** models like the Segment Anything Model (SAM) produce a mask from a point or box prompt and generalize to objects they were never explicitly trained on, pushing segmentation toward a foundation-model paradigm.

## 27.5 — Beyond boxes: keypoints, tracking, OCR, and retrieval

A handful of important tasks reshape the output rather than the backbone.

**Keypoint / pose estimation** predicts specific landmark coordinates rather than a box — the joints of a human skeleton, the corners of an eye, the facial landmarks behind a phone's face filter. The common trick is to predict a **heatmap** per keypoint (a blurry blob whose peak marks the location) instead of regressing raw $(x, y)$ numbers; heatmaps are easier to train because they give a dense, smooth gradient signal everywhere instead of a single hard target.

**Tracking** adds the time dimension: detect objects in each video frame, then link the same object across frames into a consistent track (object #7 stays #7). The classic approach (e.g. SORT) pairs a **Kalman filter** — which predicts where each object should be next from its motion — with IoU- or appearance-based matching to associate this frame's detections with last frame's tracks. The pitfall is **identity switches**, where two objects cross paths and the tracker swaps their IDs.

**OCR** (Optical Character Recognition) reads text from images and runs in two stages: *detect* text regions (a detection/segmentation problem), then *recognize* the characters in each region — typically a CNN that reads the cropped strip left-to-right feeding a sequence model with a **CTC** (Connectionist Temporal Classification) loss that aligns the predicted character stream to the label without needing per-character boxes.

**Image retrieval** flips the goal from labeling to *searching*: given a query image, find the most similar images in a large database. The backbone produces an **embedding** — a vector — for every image, and similarity is just distance in that space, so retrieval reduces to nearest-neighbor search. This is the visual cousin of text embedding search; the indexing and approximate-nearest-neighbor machinery is shared with vector search (see Chapter 32).

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  I[Image] --> B[Backbone]
  B --> C1[Classification head -> label]
  B --> C2[Detection head -> boxes]
  B --> C3[Mask head -> per-pixel mask]
  B --> C4[Keypoint head -> heatmaps]
  B --> C5[Embedding -> retrieval / tracking]
```

The recurring theme: one backbone, many heads. Choosing a vision system is mostly choosing the head and the loss that match your output shape.

## 27.6 — Vision Transformers

CNNs bake in a strong assumption: features are *local* (a filter sees a small patch) and *translation-equivariant* (the same filter slides everywhere). The **Vision Transformer (ViT)** throws that assumption out and asks whether the attention mechanism that conquered language can read images too.

The idea is disarmingly simple. Cut the image into a grid of fixed-size **patches** (say 16×16 pixels), flatten each patch and linearly project it into a vector — now each patch is a "token," exactly like a word. Add a learned **positional embedding** so the model knows where each patch sat (attention is otherwise order-blind), prepend a special classification token, and run the whole sequence through a standard Transformer encoder. The class token's final state feeds a linear classifier.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  IMG[Image] --> P[Split into 16x16 patches]
  P --> E[Flatten + linear projection]
  E --> PE[Add positional embedding]
  PE --> CLS[Prepend CLS token]
  CLS --> T[Transformer encoder]
  T --> HEAD[CLS state -> linear classifier]
```

The payoff is **global receptive field from layer one**: every patch can attend to every other patch immediately, whereas a CNN must stack many layers before distant pixels influence each other. The cost is that ViTs lack the CNN's built-in locality bias, so they are data-hungry — on small datasets a CNN often wins, but with enough data (or large-scale pretraining) ViTs match or beat CNNs and have become the default backbone for large multimodal models.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: limited data and limited compute → start with a CNN (or a CNN pretrained on ImageNet). Lots of data or a large pretrained checkpoint available → a ViT backbone usually pays off and plugs straight into multimodal pipelines.
:::

The Transformer block itself — multi-head self-attention, residual connections, layer norm — is owned by Chapter 18; ViT's only novelty is the patch-as-token tokenization of an image.

## 27.7 — Data augmentation for vision

Labeled images are expensive, and a network with millions of parameters will happily memorize a small training set. **Data augmentation** fights this by generating new training examples on the fly through label-preserving transformations: the answer "cat" doesn't change if you flip, crop, or recolor the cat, so each epoch the network sees a slightly different version and is forced to learn the invariant essence rather than memorize pixels.

The standard toolkit, roughly in order of how routinely it's used:

- **Geometric:** horizontal flip, random crop and resize, small rotations, scaling.
- **Photometric:** jitter brightness, contrast, saturation, hue; add noise or blur.
- **Occlusion:** *Cutout* / random erasing — black out a random rectangle so the model can't rely on one telltale region.
- **Mixing:** *Mixup* blends two images and their labels; *CutMix* pastes a patch of one image into another and mixes labels by area.

A crucial caveat is that the right augmentations are **task- and domain-dependent**. A horizontal flip is harmless for natural photos but corrupts a "left arrow / right arrow" classifier and is wrong for text. Aggressive color jitter can destroy a medical or satellite signal where absolute color carries meaning. Augmentation is also the engine behind much of self-supervised learning, where two augmented views of the same image are trained to agree (Chapter 19).

::: {.callout-warning}
Apply augmentation to the training set only. Augmenting your validation or test set changes what you're measuring and gives you a number that doesn't reflect real-world performance. Likewise, never apply a transformation that can flip the label (e.g. vertical flips of digits, or flips on directional/text data).
:::

## 27.8 — Key takeaways

- Classical CV hand-crafted features (SIFT, HOG) on top of gradients; deep learning replaced that step with **learned** features, fusing extraction and classification into one trained function.
- A trained CNN or ViT is a **backbone**; vision tasks differ mainly in the **head** and loss attached to it — softmax for classification, box+class for detection, per-pixel masks for segmentation, heatmaps for keypoints, embeddings for retrieval.
- Detection vocabulary you must own: **bounding box, IoU, NMS, anchors, mAP** — and the two-stage (R-CNN family) vs one-stage (YOLO, SSD) tradeoff of accuracy vs speed.
- Segmentation has three levels — **semantic** (per-pixel class), **instance** (separated objects, Mask R-CNN), **panoptic** (both) — with U-Net's encoder–decoder + skip connections as the semantic workhorse.
- **Vision Transformers** tokenize images into patches and apply self-attention for a global receptive field; data-hungry but dominant at scale and the backbone of multimodal models.
- **Augmentation** is the cheapest regularizer in vision, but transformations must preserve the label and be applied to training data only.

## 27.9 — See also

- **Chapter 16 — Convolutional Neural Networks:** the convolution, pooling, and backbone architectures this chapter builds on.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers:** the self-attention machinery underlying Vision Transformers.
- **Chapter 19 — Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning:** augmentation-driven pretraining of vision backbones without labels.
- **Chapter 32 — Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search:** the nearest-neighbor indexing behind image retrieval.
- **Chapter 34 — Multimodal AI:** how vision backbones (especially ViTs) join language models in image-text systems.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection:** the precision/recall foundations underneath mAP and mIoU.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 26 · ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory](ch26.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [28 · 💬 Natural Language Processing →](ch28.html)
